coredata.dc:title: A reliable fluorescence “turn-on” aptasensor based on dual-emitting europium metal-organic frameworks for ultrasensitive and selective detection of sulfamethazine
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Qingxiang
Hu, Xuelian
Dong, Xiaoxiao
Liu, Peng
Zhang, Ning
Gao, Zhe
Wang, Wenxiu
Li, Huiling
Wang, Shuo
Liu, Xiuying
Tang, Yiwei
coredata.dc:description: A high-performance fluorescent “turn-on” aptasensor (Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt) for sulfamethazine (SMZ) determination was designed using dual-emitting europium metal-organic frameworks (Eu-MOFs) as a signal transducer and an amplifier. Eu-MOFs featuring dual emission peaks (430 nm and 620 nm) were first prepared via a facile self-assembly strategy employing Eu (III) ions and 2-aminoterephthalic acid as precursors. The high-affinity aptamer was bonded with Eu-MOFs to form Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt through the amidation reaction. Benefiting from the integration of inherent virtues from Eu-MOFs and aptamer, the Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt-based sensor allowed sensitive and selective determination of SMZ with good linear relationships in a range of 1.4–40 ng mL−1 and a low detection line (0.379 ng mL−1). This sensor was successfully applied to the determination of trace SMZ in real samples with satisfactory recoveries (86.47–113.52%) and a relative standard deviation (<6.51). Consequently, the Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt ratiometric fluorescence sensor furnishes new possibilities for the accurate detection of various pollutants in food.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Europium metal-organic frameworks
Aptasensor
Ratiometric fluorescence detection
Sulfamethazine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of dual-emitting europium metal-organic frameworks (Eu-MOFs)
Preparation of ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor based on europium metal-organic frameworks (Eu-MOFs@SMZ-apt)
Fluorescence measurement of SMZ based on ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor
Determination of SMZ in real samples
Preparation and optimization of Eu-MOFs@SMZ-apt sensor
Ratiometric fluorescence sensing of SMZ using Eu-MOFs@SMZ-apt sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis of Eu-MOFs (a), Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt aptasensor (b), as well as electron transfer and fluorescence change in sensor preparation (c).

(a, b) SEM images of Eu-MOFs and (c) the elemental mapping images of Eu-MOFs. (d) Fluorescence emission spectra of Eu(NO3)3·6H2O, NH2-BCD, and Eu-MOFs. (e) Thermogravimetric (TGA) curve of Eu-MOFs. (f) FT-IR spectra of NH2-BCD, Eu-MOFs, and Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt. (g) Zeta potential of Eu-MOFs, aptamer and Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of Eu-MOFs, Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt, and Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt combined with SMZ. (b) UV–vis absorption spectrum of SMZ, excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt. (c) Fluorescence emission decay curves of (c) Eu-MOFs, (d) Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt alone and (e) Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt combined with SMZ.

(a) Fluorescence responses of the Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt towards different concentrations of SMZ. (b) Linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (I430/I620) of Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt sensor and SMZ concentration (1.4–40 ng mL−1). (c) The fluorescence change (ΔI) of Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt in the presence of SMZ or other antibacterial veterinary drugs. (d) The effect of ST, SP, SIZ, CLP, and OLA on the ΔI value of Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt based fluorescence sensor in the absence or presence of SMZ. (e)The effect of storage time on the ΔI value of Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt.
The detection results of SMZ in real samples by the developed method and HPLC.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: P > 0.05, manifest there were no significant difference.

head.ce:title: A reliable fluorescence “turn-on” aptasensor based on dual-emitting europium metal-organic frameworks for ultrasensitive and selective detection of sulfamethazine

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Technology, Hebei Agricultural University, Baoding 071001, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Qingxiang Liu and Xuelian Hu contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A high-performance fluorescent “turn-on” aptasensor (Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt) for sulfamethazine (SMZ) determination was designed using dual-emitting europium metal-organic frameworks (Eu-MOFs) as a signal transducer and an amplifier. Eu-MOFs featuring dual emission peaks (430 nm and 620 nm) were first prepared via a facile self-assembly strategy employing Eu (III) ions and 2-aminoterephthalic acid as precursors. The high-affinity aptamer was bonded with Eu-MOFs to form Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt through the amidation reaction. Benefiting from the integration of inherent virtues from Eu-MOFs and aptamer, the Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt-based sensor allowed sensitive and selective determination of SMZ with good linear relationships in a range of 1.4–40 ng mL−1 and a low detection line (0.379 ng mL−1). This sensor was successfully applied to the determination of trace SMZ in real samples with satisfactory recoveries (86.47–113.52%) and a relative standard deviation (<6.51). Consequently, the Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt ratiometric fluorescence sensor furnishes new possibilities for the accurate detection of various pollutants in food.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), as an up-and-coming class of multifunctional hybrid materials, have achieved the proverbial applications in the fields of gas separation (Liu et al., 2023), storage (Zhou et al., 2023), heterogeneous catalysis (Zhao & Miao, 2024), chemical sensing (Chang et al., 2023) and drug delivery (Guo, Xu, Xu, & Jia, 2023) due to the prominent superiorities (tunable structure, high porosity, and surface area). Unfortunately, it is still a challenge for the popularization in the field of sensing on account of the deficiency of signal transduction function (Huang, Li, Bai, Xiao, & Xu, 2023; Xu, Pan, Fang, & Wang, 2019). Therefore, the development of MOFs-based photoluminescence nanocomposites with unique optical properties and excellent stability will be a promising means to promote the application of MOFs.

Along with the emergence of novel fluorescent nanoparticles (quantum dots and lanthanide ions, etc.), a series of luminescent metal-organic frameworks (LMOFs) were continuously designed and exploited by encapsulating nanoparticles (the guests) into MOFs (the hosts) (Gao, Zhang, Xiao, Huang, & Wu, 2023). Especially, lanthanide metal-organic frameworks (Ln-MOFs) have been hotly utilized for chemo- and bio-sensing owing to the intrinsic luminescent properties of stable emission bands, large Stokes shift, and long fluorescence lifetimes (Hu et al., 2022; Wang, Jiang, Tissot, & Serre, 2023). However, it is noteworthy that most reported Ln-MOFs rely on the variation of a single-signal channel, which is susceptible to interference of reagent concentration, instruments, and environmental factors during the sensing process (Han et al., 2021). To address the aforementioned challenge, dual-emitting ratiometric fluorescence sensors featuring self-calibration function are proposed and taken as an efficient fluorescence strategy for a more accurate and reliable analysis. For instance, Eu-MOF, a dual-emitting europium-organic framework prepared by the self-assembly strategy of 2-aminoterephthalic acid ligand (NH2-BDC) and Eu (III) ions, was designed and applied to the ultrasensitive detection of water in organic solvents (Zhou et al., 2019). Recently, in light of the fact that organic ligands (5‑boronobezene-1, 3-dicarboxylic acid) can sensitize metal ions (Eu (III) ions) through “antenna effect”, Wang et al. (Wang et al., 2020) developed a dual-emitting lanthanide metal-organic frameworks (BA-Eu-MOF) and realized the accurate detection of Hg2+ and CH3Hg+ ions for the first time. Nevertheless, the common ratiometric fluorescence sensors based on Ln-MOFs still have certain limitations, such as poor anti-interference ability. Therefore, it is of great significance to explore novel Ln-MOFs-based ratiometric fluorescence sensors with favorable selective recognition ability for fulfilling high sensitivity and high selectivity monitoring in complex environments.

Aptamer, as a kind of single-stranded non-coding RNA, DNA, and its derivatives with specific recognition sites, can realize targeted recognition and binding with target molecules, thus enhancing the selectivity and anti-interference ability of materials (Guo et al., 2024). As effective alternatives to antibodies, aptamers have drawn extensive attention in the construction of fluorescent sensing probes because of the prominent virtues of high stability and easy modification (Ghiasi, Malekpour, & Mahpishanian, 2022). More recently, a few novel fluorescent sensors (Ln-MOFs@apt) integrating the high sensitivity of Ln-MOFs and high affinity of aptamers have been designed and developed (Wang et al., 2023; Wang, Jiang, et al., 2023; Yan, Yuan, & Yue, 2024). Wang and co-workers (Wang, Zeng, et al., 2023) proposed a reliable analysis strategy based on the combination of Ln-MOFs (Zr&Eu(2,6-NDC)) and FAM-aptamer, which was employed for the sensitive and selective measurement of pathogenic bacteria in the field environment. Therefore, the novel fluorescence strategy based on the integration of recognition units (aptamers) and signal transducers (Ln-MOFs) provides a multi-functional platform with good specificity and high sensitivity for the quality and safety control of the food industry.

Sulfamethazine (SMZ), as a sort of sulfonamides antibiotic with high-efficiency and broad-spectrum antibacterial activity, has been extensively employed for the control and therapy of various animal diseases (Li et al., 2024). However, excessive use of SMZ yields drug accumulation in the natural environment and foodstuffs, which will eventually pose a series of adverse effects (teratogenicity and antibiotic resistance) to humans through food chain enrichment (Song et al., 2022; Yan, Yuan, & Yue, 2024; Yan et al., 2024). To protect the health of consumers, many countries have promulgated the maximum residue limit (MRL) standard of SMZ. The MRL of SMZ in animal products is set as 100 ng g−1 by Canada (Wang, Gong, Zhu, Wang, & Geng, 2021). According to the standard of China, the MRL for SMZ in animal products is specified as 100 μg kg−1, except for milk (25 μg kg−1). Long term consumption of food containing sulfonamide residues, even if not exceeding the MRL, may lead to the accumulation of sulfonamide in the body, posing a serious threat to physical health. Therefore, it is urgently desired to develop an efficient and accurate detection stratagem for the SMZ analysis, which is of great significance for monitoring environmental water pollution and food safety. At present, many mature analytical strategies have been utilized for the detection of SMZ, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) (Chung, Lee, Chung, & Lee, 2009), electrochemical (Alagarsamy, Mariappan, Chen, & Sakthinathan, 2023), immunological methods (Wang et al., 2022), and fluorescence methods (Marimuthu, Arumugam, Sabarinathan, Li, & Chen, 2021). Among the aforementioned approaches, fluorescence methods exhibit great prospects in the sensitive detection of residual veterinary drugs because of the virtue of immediate response, simple operation, and low cost. However, reported fluorescence sensors for the detection of SMZ are still constructed based on fluorescence strategies of monotonous quenching or enhancement. To our knowledge, the dual-emitting fluorescence sensor based on the integration of lanthanide metal-organic frameworks and aptamers for specific recognition and ratiometric fluorescence detection of SMZ has never been explored.

Inspired by the research and analysis, a valid fluorescence strategy based on dual-emitting europium metal-organic frameworks (Eu-MOFs) conjugated to aptamers was proposed for highly sensitive and selective analysis of SMZ. In this strategy, Eu-MOFs featuring dual emission peaks (430 nm and 620 nm) were first prepared by self-assembly of Eu3+ and 2-aminoterephthalic acid (NH2-BDC). As the recognition element, the high-affinity aptamer was introduced and bonded with Eu-MOFs to form Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt sensor through the chemical coupling method. Benefiting from the combination of Eu-MOFs and aptamers, the as-constructed Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt sensor demonstrated superior stability, ultrasensitive fluorescence response, and desirable recognition performance for SMZ. More significantly, Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt sensor was triumphantly applied to the determination of SMZ in actual samples with satisfactory results.

Europium (III) nitrate hexahydrate (Eu(NO3)3•6H2O) was purchased from Tianjin Heowns Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China, http://www.heowns.com/). SMZ aptamer with the following sequence 5′-NH2-(CH2)6-TTA GCT TAT GCG TTG GCC GGG ATA AGG ATC CAG CCG TTG TAG ATT TGC GTT CTA ACT CTC-3′ (Wu & Cui, 2019) was provided by Shanghai Xianding Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China, https://www.9dingchem.com/). N-hydroxy succinimide (NHS), 1-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-3-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), 2-aminoterephthalic acid (NH2-BDC), sulfamethazine (SMZ), sulfathiazole (ST), sulfapyridine (SP), sulfisoxazole (SIZ), chloramphenicol (CLP), and olaquindox (OLA) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China, https://www.aladdin-e.com/). The fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra of materials were recorded on the F-320 (Guangdong Sci. &Tech., China) and UV-2800 A spectrophotometer (Unico, China), respectively. Functional groups of materials were measured on a Nicolet Nexus 670 Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Nicolet, USA) by KBr pellet-based method. The morphology and elemental composition of materials were observed by TESCAN MIRA LMS scanning electron microscopy (SEM, TESCAN, Czech Republic). The zeta potential analysis was performed on a Zetasizer Nano ZS ZEN3600 potential analysis instrument (Malvern, UK).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of dual-emitting europium metal-organic frameworks (Eu-MOFs)
ce:section.ce:para: Dual-emitting europium metal-organic frameworks were prepared according to the previously reported self-assembly strategy with minor modifications (Zhang et al., 2018). In brief, Eu(NO3)3•6H2O (446 mg) and NH2-BDC (271.7 mg) were first dispersed in a 100 mL autoclave containing DMF (15 mL) and water (15 mL) and then thoroughly mixed with acetic acid (100 μL). Subsequently, the mixture was placed at 90 °C for 18 h. After cooling down to room temperature in ambient air, the resulting mixed solution was washed with DMF and ethanol to remove unreacted precursors. Finally, Eu-MOFs were attained through drying at 60 °C for 12 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor based on europium metal-organic frameworks (Eu-MOFs@SMZ-apt)
ce:section.ce:para: Ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor based on europium metal-organic frameworks were prepared by the literature method (He, Zhang, & Fan, 2018; Li et al., 2023). First, as-synthesized Eu-MOFs were dispersed in HEPES buffer (0.9 mg·mL−1) and activated by introducing EDC (4.5 mg) and NHS (7.5 mg). After stirring at room temperature for 2 h, the mixed solution was centrifuged, and the collected precipitate was washed 3 times with distilled water to remove unreacted activators. Subsequently, the activated Eu-MOFs were redispersed in HEPES buffer containing 0.45 μM aptamer and stirred continuously for 6 h at 4 °C. Finally, Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt was obtained by centrifuging and rinsing 3 times with HEPES buffer, and stored at 4 °C for subsequent experiments. As a contrast system, Eu-MOFs/SMZ-Apt sensor based on physical adsorption strategy was prepared under above same process, except that no the activators EDC and NHS were introduced.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence measurement of SMZ based on ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: In the analysis of SMZ solutions with different concentrations by fluorescence procedure, Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt suspension was prepared by mixing Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt (0.9 mg) and ethanol (1 mL) in a 2 mL centrifuge tube. After that, different concentrations of SMZ standard solutions (1.4, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, and 40 ng mL−1) were added to the mixed solution of Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt. The fluorescence spectra in the range of 400–750 nm were measured at the excitation wavelength of 382 nm after incubation at 25 °C for 40 min with a mixed oscillator. The selectivity of Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt sensor to SMZ was estimated by introducing antibacterial veterinary drugs related to SMZ structure into the detection system. Three repeated measurements were conducted in every experiment. To appraise the sensing performance of the as-prepared Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt sensor, the difference value (ΔI) determined by the equation (ΔI = I r - I 0) was employed as a measurement index, where I 0 and I r represent the fluorescence intensity ratio (I430/I620) of the sensor in the absence and presence of SMZ respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of SMZ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the applicability of Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt sensor, milk, shrimp, pork liver, chicken purchased from local supermarkets, and tap water obtained from the laboratory were chosen as real samples. The real sample pretreatment was carried out according to the following process. Firstly, real samples (5 g or 5 mL) were added to a 50 mL centrifuge tube. Subsequently, an appropriate amount of SMZ standard solution was spiked into real samples. After mixing evenly, the samples were extracted twice by introducing acetonitrile aqueous solution (25 mL, 100:3, v/v) and acetonitrile (25 mL), respectively. The collected two-part supernatants were then mixed with 0.1 mol/L hydrochloric acid solution (4 mL) and evaporated at 40 °C until it was nearly dry. After that, the extraction solution of the above samples was treated twice with 6 mL n-hexane to remove fat and dried with a nitrogen stream. Finally, the treated samples were fixed in 5 mL ethanol and filtered using a 0.45 μM microfiltration membrane for the subsequent detection. Three repeated experiments were performed to ensure the reliability of the results.

For HPLC analysis, the corresponding sample extraction, purification, and detection procedures were designed and implemented according to the standard of China (GB 29694–2013). The HPLC detection system was mainly composed of a C18 reverse-phase column, a UV–vis detector, and a workstation for data collection. The mobile phase was 0.1% formic acid and acetonitrile. The column temperature was set at 30 °C. And the injection volume was 20 μL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and optimization of Eu-MOFs@SMZ-apt sensor
ce:section.ce:para: A high-performance fluorescent “turn-on” aptasensor (Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt) was constructed based on dual-emitting europium metal-organic frameworks and incorporating aptamer. As illustrated in Fig. 1a, europium metal-organic frameworks (Eu-MOFs) were first prepared via a facile self-assembly strategy using Eu (III) ions and 2-aminoterephthalic acid (NH2-BDC) as the precursors. Subsequently, Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt sensor (Fig. 1b) was constructed via an amidation reaction between the amino group (inherent in aptamer sequences) and the carboxyl group (free on the surface of Eu-MOFs). Herein, the as-prepared Eu-MOFs exhibited two emission peaks corresponding to characteristic fluorescence (FL) peaks of NH2-BDC (430 nm) and Eu (III) ions (620 nm) under 382 nm excitation because the coordinated NH2-BDC ligands can sensitize the fluorescence of Eu (III) ions through antenna effect. After the introduction of aptamers, Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt triggered a significant fluorescence decrease of the ligand NH2-BDC due to the electron transfer between the aptamer (the electron donor) and the NH2-BDC (the electron acceptor). In the presence of SMZ, Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt can combine with SMZ through hydrogen bonding and van der Waals force (Duan et al., 2023). Meanwhile, electrons derived from the aptamer were transferred to SMZ because SMZ had the stronger electron-withdrawing group (benzenesulfonamide group), leading to the recovery of NH2-BDC emission peak in Eu-MOFs (Fig. 1c). As an internal parameter, the fluorescence intensity of Eu (III) ions remained unchanged during the detection process. Therefore, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt furnished a promising strategy for the high-performance evaluation of detecting SMZ.

Simultaneously, to attain Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt sensor with well-pleasing specificity and sensitivity for SMZ, the predominant conditions were optimized (Fig. S1 and S2), involving the addition amount of acetic acid, reaction time, synthesis temperature, and the molar ratio of Eu (III) ions to NH2-BDC in the preparation of Eu-MOFs, as well as the concentration of Eu-MOFs, incubation time, and the concentration of aptamer in the preparation of Eu-MOFs coupling aptamer.

The morphology of synthesized Eu-MOFs was observed and recorded utilizing SEM images. As delineated in Fig. 2
a and b , fluorescent materials Eu-MOFs prepared by self-assembly of NH2-BDC and Eu (III) ions presented a sheet crystal structure with extraordinarily high porosity. The elemental mapping images (Fig. 2c) exhibited that characteristic elements (C, O, N, and Eu) were uniformly distributed in Eu-MOFs, which preliminarily manifested the successful synthesis of Eu-MOFs. Meanwhile, photoluminescence properties of Eu-MOFs were demonstrated by comparing the fluorescence spectra of NH2-BDC and Eu(NO3)3·6H2O. As presented in Fig. 2d, Eu-MOFs exhibited both the emission peak of the ligand (about 430 nm) and the characteristic emission peaks (590–710 nm) derived from the transition of Eu (III) ions (5D0 → 7FJ, J = 1–4) (Song, Zhu, & Hong, 2023). In addition, compared with the fluorescence spectra of NH2-BDC and Eu(NO3)3·6H2O, the as-prepared Eu-MOFs displayed stronger fluorescence emission peaks, which was attributed to the decrease of non-radiative relaxation and the sensitization of NH2-BDC acting as an “antenna” to Eu (III) ions. The aforementioned results implied that Eu-MOFs with dual emission (430 and 620 nm) were triumphantly constructed. It can be seen from TGA curve (Fig. 2e) that the degradation process of Eu-MOFs can be primarily divided into two stages. During 137–345 °C, the initial stage weight loss of Eu-MOFs corresponded to the removal of solvent molecule DMF and the decomposition of a few ligands (Zhang et al., 2023). After that, the weight loss of Eu-MOFs was ascribed to the further fracture of ligands (Wang, Jiang, et al., 2023; Wang, Zeng, et al., 2023). In addition, the final residue of Eu-MOFs at 1000 °C was 54.3%, revealing that Eu-MOFs enjoyed the high char formation characteristics (Zhang et al., 2023). The thermogravimetric analysis results indicated that Eu-MOFs featured the thermal degradation characteristics of the metal-organic framework and favorable thermal stability.

As shown in Fig. S3a, the diffraction peaks of Eu-MOFs are basically matches with the simulated XRD pattern of Eu-MOFs (CCDC-1063110), indicating good phase purity (Xia, Sun, Cheng, Xia, & Yin, 2022). However, a few diffraction peaks that are not very matched are found, which may be resulted from the presence of small amounts of impurities in the material (Lei, Zhang, Liu, Dai, & Zhao, 2022). The chemical state and bonding configuration of Eu-MOFs were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As shown in Fig. S3b, the full survey spectra verified the presence of Eu, N, O, and C elements in Eu-MOFs, which agreed with the EDS results. In the high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. S3c), three split peaks observed at 288.6 eV, 286.0 eV, and 284.8 eV are found, which are attributed to carboxylate carbon, CN bond, and carbon of the benzene, respectively (Wang et al., 2021). The spectra of N 1 s in Fig. S3d showed two kinds of binding energy, the peak at 400.8 eV was related to NH bonds and the peak at 399.4 eV was arisen from CN bonds. The N 1 s and C 1 s high-resolution spectrum proved that the BDC-NH2 ligands were successfully introduced into the MOFs. The spectra of O 1 s in Fig. S3d showed three kinds of binding energy at 532.3, 532.1 eV, and 531.3 eV, which were assigned to CO single bond, the O-C=O from the carboxylate group, and the EuO bond (Shi, Zhang, Wang, Xue, & Chen, 2022), respectively. The high-resolution spectrum of Eu 3d was deconvoluted into five peaks (Fig. S3f). One peak at 1144.2 eV was a satellite signal (abbreviated as “Sat.”), and the other two peaks of 1165.6 eV and 1136.0 eV were responsible for trivalent Eu. The presence of trace amounts of the divalent Eu was identified by the peaks at 1157.5 eV and 1126.0 eV, which originate from the surface layer, called the “surface valence transition” phenomenon (Song et al., 2024). These results all indicated Eu metal forms MOF with NH2-BDC.

The FT-IR spectroscopy was performed to determine functional groups of materials. As delineated in Fig. 2f, a wide absorption peak (2500–3000 cm−1) corresponding to the vibration of the OH bond on the carboxyl group of NH2-BDC was not observed in the FT-IR spectrum of Eu-MOFs (Wu et al., 2023). However, the characteristic absorption peaks derived from the stretching vibrations of -COO− (1542 and 1437 cm−1) and free -NH2 (3363 and 3462 cm−1) in ligands emerged on Eu-MOFs curve, implying that Eu (III) center chelated with the deprotonated ligand (-COO− group) to form the novel metal-organic frameworks Eu-MOFs (Wang, Jiang, et al., 2023; Wang, Zeng, et al., 2023). Compared with the Eu-MOFs curve, the characteristic peaks at 1658 cm−1 and 1257 cm−1 associated with the stretching vibration of the CO bond and CN bond in the amide group were significantly enhanced on the curve of Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt. Furthermore, a new absorption peak representing the stretching vibration of the NH bond (3213 cm−1) in the amide group appeared in Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt. These results indicated that a novel fluorescent sensor based on europium metal-organic frameworks conjugated to the aptamer of SMZ was successfully synthesized. As portrayed in Fig. 2g, the zeta potential of the sensor displayed a fluctuation from 6.89 mV to −3.15 mV after combining the aptamer with negative charge, which further verified that the aptamer was successfully decorated on the surface of Eu-MOFs (Duan et al., 2023).

The feasibility of the developed fluorescence sensor was explored by monitoring the fluorescence spectra before and after binding to the target molecule SMZ. As illustrated in Fig. 3a, Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt maintained the unique dual emission peaks of Eu-MOFs. However, in the presence of SMZ, Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt yielded a conspicuous fluorescence enhancement at 430 nm, which confirmed that Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt sensor possessed a sensitive fluorescence response to SMZ. Meanwhile, the fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra of Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt and Eu-MOFs/SMZ-Apt prepared by the chemical coupling and physical adsorption were investigated (Fig. S4). Compared with Eu-MOFs/SMZ-Apt, Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt displayed a more significant fluorescence response and UV absorption. A possible reason is that the Eu-MOFs material could not bind more adapters by physical adsorption. These results implied that the as-constructed Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt sensor based on the chemical coupling approach had good feasibility for the accurate analysis of SMZ.

The possible fluorescence sensing mechanism of Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt sensor was discussed and enunciated. Generally speaking, fluorescence enhancement was primarily ascribed to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or photoinduced electron transfer (PET). As displayed in Fig. 3b, there was no spectral overlapping between the UV–vis absorption spectrum of SMZ and the fluorescence emission spectrum of Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt, eliminating the possibility of fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) (Duan et al., 2023; Zhu, Yuan, Han, Liu, & Sun, 2021). However, there was partially overlapped between the excitation spectrum of Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt and the UV absorption spectrum of SMZ. Thus, it was speculated that the fluorescence enhancement Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt combined with SMZ was attributed to PET (Cao et al., 2021). Herein, Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt was synthesized via an amidation reaction between the ligand of Eu-MOFs (carboxyl group) and aptamer (amino group). The aptamer acted as the donor can transfer the electrons to the empty orbital of the acceptor Eu-MOFs with the electron-withdrawing group (carboxyl group) under photoexcitation, inhibiting the direct transition of excited state electrons to the original ground state in the ligand. Therefore, after bonding the aptamer, the fluorescence intensity at 430 nm of the sensor was quenched. However, in the presence of SMZ, Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt can combine with SMZ through hydrogen bonding and van der Waals force (Duan et al., 2023). SMZ with stronger electron withdrawing ability (benzenesulfonamide group) can capture the aptamer electrons originally transferred to Eu-MOFs, leading to the recovery of fluorescence intensity of Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt. Additionally, the sensing mechanism of the sensor was further verified by observing the fluorescence emission decay curves of Eu-MOFs, Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt alone and Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt combined with SMZ ( Fig. 3
c, d and e). Thus, photoinduced electron transfer (PET) can be considered as a possible mechanism to explain the fluorescence “turn-on” of the sensor.

Aiming at achieving high sensitivity determination of SMZ, the related sensing conditions were optimized, including reaction solvent, reaction temperature, and response time. As revealed in Fig. S5a, the effects of different reaction solvents on the sensing system were investigated. Evidently, the fluorescence response of Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt sensor towards SMZ achieved a peak in EtOH. As an important parameter, the reaction temperature was optimized by monitoring the fluorescence change of Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt (Fig. S5b). The sensing system based on Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt demonstrated the strongest fluorescence response to SMZ at 25 °C. Along with the extension of incubation time from 0 to 60 min, the I430/I620 value of Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt first increased and then achieved a plateau at 40 min, meaning that the reaction reached equilibrium at 40 min (Fig. S5c and S5d). In summary, the optimal reaction solvent, reaction temperature, and response time were fixed at EtOH, 25 °C, and 40 min, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescence sensing of SMZ using Eu-MOFs@SMZ-apt sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Under the aforementioned optimum sensing conditions, the sensing performance of Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt sensor towards SMZ was investigated by exposing the sensor to SMZ standard solutions with different concentrations (1.4, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40 ng mL−1). As portrayed in Fig. 4a, the concentration-dependent fluorescence spectra manifested that the characteristic fluorescence emission peak (430 nm) of the sensor was progressively enhanced with the increase of SMZ concentration. However, the emission fluorescence peak (620 nm) of the sensor remained unchanged under the same excitation. As depicted in Fig. 4b, there was an excellent linear relationship between the fluorescence response (I430/I620) of Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt sensor and SMZ concentration in the range of 1.4–40 ng mL−1. The corresponding limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.379 ng mL−1 according to the standard 3σ/S (Hu et al., 2023; Tawfik et al., 2020). As depicted in Table S1, compared with the previously reported approaches for the determination of SMZ, the presented method based on Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt sensor displayed a lower detection limit and a comparable linear range. The before-mentioned results manifested that the proposed Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt with superior sensing performance was highly suitable for the sensitive detection of SMZ.

The selectivity of as-established Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt was assessed by comparing the fluorescence response of the sensor in the presence of SMZ or several other antibacterial veterinary drugs with similar structures (ST, SP, SIZ, CLP, and OLA). The chemical structure of SMZ and its structural analogs was provided in Fig. S6. As presented in Fig. 4c, only SMZ enabled Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt sensor to yield the more prominent fluorescence change, while other antibacterial veterinary drugs triggered a tiny influence on the fluorescence response of the sensor. The above comparison results revealed that the Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt sensor displayed remarkable affinity and recognition ability to SMZ.

To further explore the specific binding ability of Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt sensor for SMZ in a complex environment, ST, SP, SIZ, CLP, and OLA were selected as potential coexisting antibacterial veterinary drugs for competitive experiments. As depicted in Fig. 4d, the fluorescence change of these interfering analytes to Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt sensor was similar to that of the blank. However, upon the introduction of SMZ, the ΔI value of the detection system increased significantly, indicating that potential coexisting analytes had a negligible effect on the detection of SMZ. The superior selectivity of Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt sensor was ascribed to the existence of complementary base sites with SMZ on the sensor surface, which contributed to the selective binding of the sensor to SMZ and inhibited the nonspecific adsorption of the sensor to structural analogs. Therefore, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor based on Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt exhibited satisfactory specificity for SMZ even in complex environments where structural analogs coexisted.

As the essential sensing performance, the stability of the sensor was appraised by recording the fluorescence change of Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt under different storage time. As illustrated in Fig. 4e, the fluorescence data of Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt sensor displayed an insignificant reduction within 35 days, underlining that Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt sensor enjoyed superior storage stability.

The practicability of the as-developed fluorescent detection method based on Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt sensor was appraised by selecting milk, shrimp, pork liver, chicken, and tap water as practical samples. No SMZ was found in the aforementioned samples, so the standard recovery experience was conducted by introducing different concentrations of SMZ standard solution into real samples. The corresponding experimental results are calculated and presented in Table 1 . For the fluorescence sensing method based on Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt sensor, satisfactory recoveries of 86.47–113.52% and RSD of <6.51% were obtained. The P value of the t-test revealed that there was no significant difference in recoveries between the fluorescence sensing method and HPLC (P > 0.05). These results implied that the developed method based on Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt sensor can be considered as one of the promising tools for the determination of SMZ.

In conclusion, a novel ratiometric fluorescence sensor (Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt) based on aptamer functionalized europium metal-organic frameworks was successfully constructed and achieved SMZ detection through efficient photoinduced electron transfer. Thanks to the combination of europium metal-organic frameworks and aptamer, the as-established Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt sensor demonstrated several appealing superiorities. First of all, the unique photoluminescence properties of europium metal-organic frameworks enabled the sensor to exhibit an outstanding double response fluorescence signal, enhancing the accuracy and sensitivity of the sensor in monitoring SMZ. Secondly, the introduction of Eu-MOFs with high porosity and large surface area was favorable for rapid and high-efficiency bonding of SMZ, realizing signal amplification. Thirdly, the decoration of aptamer with high affinity endowed the sensor with superior anti-interference ability. Benefiting from prominent advantages, the proposed sensor exhibited a low limit of detection (0.379 ng mL−1), a linear range (1.4–40 ng mL−1), and superior selectivity. More importantly, the application of the proposed method was verified by utilizing HPLC in spiked milk, shrimp, pork liver, chicken, and tap water with satisfactory results. Consequently, Eu-MOFs@SMZ-Apt sensor furnishes a promising avenue for the accurate determination of various pollutants in food.

Qingxiang Liu: Writing – original draft, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xuelian Hu: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Xiaoxiao Dong: Validation, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Peng Liu: Validation, Software, Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis. Ning Zhang: Validation, Resources, Methodology, Formal analysis. Zhe Gao: Validation, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis. Wenxiu Wang: Validation, Software, Formal analysis. Huiling Li: Resources, Investigation, Data curation. Shuo Wang: Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis. Xiuying Liu: Validation, Resources. Yiwei Tang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32302194) and the Scientific Research Foundation for the Introduced Talent of Hebei Agricultural University (No. YJ2023017 and No. YJ201911).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2024.139756.
